data.title: Natural Gas: $3 Support Under Pressure As Breakdown Risk Builds
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

​U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) are hovering near $3 per MMBtu, holding just above a rising long-term trendline that has guided the market since late summer 2025. The contract has steadied after the sharp spike toward the $7 area earlier this year, but the latest consolidation has taken on a defensive tone as sellers continue to control the broader technical structure.

The market’s current posture reflects a tug-of-war between lingering downside pressure and early signs of stabilization at a long-watched support zone. With weather-driven catalysts limited and broader energy pricing softer, the next move may be dictated as much by positioning and chart levels as by fundamentals.

Chart structure weakens after the winter spike

Technically, natural gas remains under strain. Price is trading beneath all major moving averages, with the 20-day EMA near $3.5, the 50-day around $3.8, the 100-day near $3.8, and the 200-day close to $3.7. That stacked band between roughly $3.5 and $3.8 represents an overhead supply zone that has repeatedly capped rebounds. Any recovery is likely to face selling unless buyers can reclaim the 20-day EMA and then sustain traction into the heavier-resistance area.

Bollinger Bands widened sharply during the earlier surge toward $7, then began to compress as volatility cooled. Prices have drifted into the lower half of the band structure, signaling persistent downside pressure without a clear capitulation signal. Momentum indicators appear fragile rather than fully exhausted, suggesting rallies may remain corrective until a stronger reversal pattern develops.

The key near-term line in the sand is the rising trendline support around $2.90 to $3.00. A clean daily close below that area would represent a technical breakdown and could accelerate selling toward $2.7, followed by $2.5. On the upside, resistance is layered at $3.3 first, then more decisively at $3.5 and $3.8. A move back above $3.8 would be the clearest signal that bullish control is returning.

Macro tone softens as gas trades more on balance than headlines

On the fundamental side, the picture is mixed. Reuters reporting on Poland’s Orlen highlighted stronger refining performance in the fourth quarter, aided by shifts in diesel trade flows linked to sanctions and disruptions. But the broader commodity complex has cooled, with Brent crude down nearly 15% and natural gas prices also retreating from year-ago highs.

That divergence reinforces the idea that natural gas is currently trading more on supply-demand balance and technical positioning than on a single bullish catalyst. Without a renewed weather shock or a major geopolitical disruption, rebounds may struggle to turn into a lasting uptrend.

As previously discussed, natural gas has repeatedly snapped back after weather-driven spikes, only to mean-revert once demand premiums fade. The market is again nearing a decision point. Holding above $2.90 keeps the consolidation intact, but a break below that trendline would likely reset expectations for the next leg lower.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: The Hidden Math Behind Ares Capital Beating Oaktree Specialty Lending
data.description:
At a time when the business development company sector (BIZD) is in a sharp downturn, it's important to take a step back and keep one's vision on what is most important to long-term value creation in the sector. While investors are fretting about the risks of software disruption from AI hurting BDCs with software loans in their portfolios, and many others are fretting about dividend cut announcements, there are more fundamental building blocks that need to be kept in mind when determining what value a company brings to the table for the long term.

Comparing two prominent BDCs, namely Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) and Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL), investors can see this hidden math at work quite clearly. Over the past five years, ARCC has thoroughly outperformed OCSL, and that separation has been particularly pronounced over the past two years.

While some would attribute it to ARCC's price-to-earnings ratio or book value ratio compressing by less than OCSL has over that period of time, there is clearly much more to the equation.

Ultimately, while price-to-book value movements can impact total returns, especially over the short term, over the long term, it is the economic total return that matters most. Namely, the total dividends per share paid out and the total change in the net asset value per share. This is all that matters because a company pays out cash to shareholders via dividends, and all of the remaining cash flow that it retains is used to either build its NAV via reinvesting in new investments, repurchasing shares, or paying down debt.

In this article, we will take a look at the real economic return from both companies and then show why ARCC has so handily outperformed OCSL. From there, we can take a look at why ARCC is likely to continue outperforming OCSL on an economic return basis moving forward.

Economic Total Return: The Only Metric That Truly Matters

From the chart below, we can see that ARCC has been much better at preserving book value per share over time, as it has actually improved its book value per share from $17.46 five years ago to $19.94 today, while OCSL has seen its book value per share decline quite precipitously from $21.27 per share to just $16.30 per share today.

That means that ARCC has grown its book value per share by 14.18% over a five-year period, which amounts to a 2.7% book value per share CAGR, while OCSL has seen its book value per share decline by 23.35% over that period of time.

Meanwhile, ARCC has had a total of $9.25 in dividends, and OCSL has paid out $7.525 per share in dividends over that period of time. What that means is that OCSL's total economic return over the past five years is a measly 12%, or just 2.3% on an annualized basis, whereas ARCC's comes in at a very strong 67.2%, or, on an annualized basis, 10.8%.

While OCSL has delivered substantial dividend payouts over the past decade, it did slash its dividend over that period of time, which was a reflection of its rapidly declining net asset value per share, whereas ARCC has been able to sustain its base dividend over that period of time, even in the face of falling short-term interest rates, thanks to its stable and even increasing net asset value per share.

Underwriting Quality and NAV Stability: Where the Real Gap Emerges

Ultimately, the reason ARCC has outperformed OCSL so much has little to do with the headline numbers like dividends per share or even NII per share in any given quarter. Rather, it has had much to do with the underlying underwriting quality of the portfolio and its ability to preserve the value of its holdings over time.

A clear symptom of this is the change in OCSL's investor presentations over the years, as early on in Oaktree's ownership tenure of the fund (2019-2021), when its underwriting performance was much stronger, it highlighted the fact that it had low to no nonaccruals. Meanwhile, today, you cannot even find nonaccruals mentioned in its investor presentation, nor does it even have internal risk rating metrics highlighted in it.

Moreover, if you look at its net asset value per share bridge, you can see what has become an all-too-common trend, namely a substantial amount of adjusted net realized and unrealized losses each quarter, with $0.37 per share of unrealized losses and $0.02 per share of net realized gains during the quarter, which far more than overwhelmed the $0.01 of surplus-adjusted NII above the quarterly distribution to lead to a substantial $0.34 per share NAV decline during the quarter.

With non-accruals representing 3.1% of the total debt portfolio at fair value, the company is still dealing with an elevated number of issues, especially given that it has a total of 11 investments on non-accrual and added one additional holding in the portfolio on the non-accrual list during the quarter.

ARCC, meanwhile, gives much greater insight into portfolio non-accruals with a whole slide dedicated to it in its investor presentation that shows that it has kept them consistently low, with them sitting at 1.2% on a fair value basis and 1.8% on an amortized cost basis, both of which are well within their historical ranges and are on the low end relative to peers in the sector.

Not only that, but it also provides considerable insight into its portfolio performance quality with illustrations depicting the performance on a percentage basis by fair value of its holdings, with its best-performing holdings at grades 4 and 3 sitting at levels consistent with what it has achieved in recent quarters and making up a whopping 96% of the portfolio, with just 2% at grade 2 and 2% at the riskiest grade 1 levels.

This means that a spike in non-accruals to a level that would be generally damaging to the company's NAV per share is highly unlikely in the near future. This is reflected in the company's fairly stable net asset value per share in recent quarters, as highlighted earlier in the article. In fact, on a year-over-year basis, the net asset value per share has actually increased from $19.89 to $19.94, highlighting that it is not suffering these unrecognized and recognized losses quarter after quarter that OCSL is.

Software Exposure, AI Fears, and Balance Sheet Flexibility

Looking ahead, ARCC remains well-positioned to deliver value for shareholders for several reasons. First of all, the company feels good about its dividend, which today yields about 10%, as net investment income per share of $0.52 in the most recent quarter comfortably covered the $0.48 per share dividend during the quarter to the tune of a 1.08x coverage ratio. On top of that, the balance sheet has sufficient flexibility, with a net debt-to-equity ratio of just 1.08x at the end of the quarter, which gives it significant flexibility to move toward the upper end of a 1.25x target leverage range to further bolster net investment income per share to support the dividend.

The company also sees incremental growth opportunities through its senior direct lending program and Ivy Hill Asset Management joint venture, which could deliver enhanced NII per share. Finally, the company's credit portfolio remains in strong shape, as I already mentioned, which means that it should not have to suffer steep losses that would erode its NII per share.

It is also worth noting that the company, despite its 24% software exposure, feels confident about its positioning in the software space. ARCC emphasized on its latest earnings call that its software portfolio is deliberately positioned to be resistant to technological disruption, including AI. For example, its holdings are concentrated in foundational, mission-critical infrastructure software companies that sit at the core of their customers' technology stacks. Given the deeply embedded nature of these software programs in their clients' operations, they have high switching costs and would also bring significant time and risk to be replaced by newly created AI solutions.

In particular, this is because it targets industries like healthcare and financial services, which tend to be very risk-averse and compliance- and regulation-heavy, where they regularly get audited. Additionally, the companies it invests in tend to have considerable proprietary databases that they have accumulated over the years, as well as established customer relationships that give them a competitive moat against potential AI-enabled competitors. Additionally, it has diversified its software portfolio across customers and industries, which means that even if one or two of them were to suffer AI disruption, it would have only a minimal impact from an overall standpoint.

Last but not least, these loans tend to sit at the top of the capital structure with very low loan-to-values, and their customers right now are very healthy in aggregate, with faster EBITDA growth in the overall portfolio and LTVs of roughly 37%, giving them a substantial equity cushion in the face of potential AI-related impairments.

While OCSL does have some strengths going for it as well, including a 1.07x net debt-to-equity ratio, which means it still has considerable flexibility to add additional debt to support its dividend, and it also has a defensive posture with an 85% portfolio allocation of first-lien senior secured loans and only 15% exposure to software and services, it is still facing some challenges. For example, in addition to its elevated non-accruals and weak net asset value per share performance, its adjusted NII of $0.41 per share in the quarter barely covered its dividend of $0.40 per share during the quarter.

Additionally, its software loans are not quite as defensively positioned, with an average loan-to-value of 47%, which is a full 1,000 basis points higher than ARCC's, while the median EBITDA is only $176 million compared to the $350 million average EBITDA for ARCC. Nonetheless, it is also pursuing similar targets in its underwriting for software businesses, so we do not anticipate a big issue from its software portfolio. Meanwhile, both businesses have pretty well-staggered debt maturities, which should prevent them from being too big a headwind on either front.

Why One is a Trade and the Other a Long-term Compounder

The final point to keep in mind is that OCSL currently trades at a big discount to its net asset value of 26.32%, whereas ARCC trades at a much more modest discount of 3.21% below its net asset value. Thus, while both businesses appear to be slightly undervalued in my view, OCSL has a much greater margin of safety priced in to account for its weaker underwriting performance.

I rate both companies as a buy right now, as I do not think that the discount for OCSL is fully warranted, given that it has been making some progress on its loans, Oaktree is a reputable player in the high-yield credit space, and it does have some wiggle room on its balance sheet to support NII per share in the face of further potential interest rate cuts, as well as the fact that its relatively small software exposure could be considered a plus in the current environment. However, I think that over the long term, ARCC's track record and current underwriting strength position it to deliver a superior economic total return to OCSL.

Thus, I would view the purchase of OCSL as more of a trade that is betting on a material closing of the price-to-NAV discount before selling it and recycling the capital into another undervalued opportunity. Meanwhile, ARCC's strong underlying economic total return engine that combines consistent dividend payments with a sustainable and even growing NAV per share over time makes it a better bet for a long-term buy-and-hold equity income position for risk-averse investors.

Given the reality that economic total return has such an outsized impact on total returns over time, we are willing to pay higher price-to-NAV multiples for quality, proven long-term winners like ARCC in the BDC space, especially in the current environment where concerns are elevated and various CEOs have acknowledged a challenging environment for private credit.

At the same time, we are not completely blind to valuation and opportunistically invest in deeply undervalued BDCs in our core portfolio at High Yield Investor. By striking an effective balance between risk, quality, yield, and valuation, we have been able to significantly outperform the market over time with our 7%+ yield, below-market beta portfolios, including past positions in OCSL and ARCC, and both remain on our watch list today.

data.title: Uranium's Next Decade: From Tight Supply To A Broader Mining Boom
data.description:
Global nuclear power is enjoying a renewed moment as governments and corporates search for reliable, low-carbon electricity to meet rising energy demand. The surge in power consumption driven by AI, data centers, and electrification is reshaping long-term energy planning and pushing nuclear back into the center of the investment debate.

The International Atomic Energy Agency projects that global nuclear capacity could double by 2050, reaching between 561 gigawatts (low case) and 992 gigawatts (high case). Policy support is also strengthening. In May 2025, the US issued a series of executive orders aimed at quadrupling domestic nuclear capacity to 400 gigawatts by 2050, from roughly 100 gigawatts today. Other countries, including China, India, Russia, Turkey, and South Africa have also outlined expansion plans as they look to reduce dependence on fossil fuels and secure stable baseload generation.

Corporate demand is adding momentum. Over 2023 and 2024, several hyperscalers including Meta Platforms (META), Amazon's AWS (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Oracle (ORCL), and Equinix (EQIX) have announced a wave of nuclear-related initiatives, including power purchase agreements and equity investments in nuclear start-ups, signaling that big tech is increasingly willing to back atomic energy as a long-term solution.

This shift has brought uranium, the essential fuel for nuclear reactors, sharply back into focus. Prices have risen as demand tightens, and utilities move to lock in longer-term supply contracts at higher levels. Conditions are increasingly in place for a multi-year uranium upcycle, with benefits extending across miners, developers and the wider nuclear fuel chain.

Visible Alpha consensus forecasts suggest the industry is moving from today’s tight, concentrated supply base toward a broader and more competitive landscape.

Aggregate uranium revenue across 11 listed global producers covered by Visible Alpha is estimated to climb from $4.7 billion in 2023 to $14.9 billion by 2033. Growth is strongest in the second half of the decade as new mines enter production. The revenue trajectory reflects both rising volumes and improving realized pricing.

Average realized uranium prices across the group are forecast to rise from $59.6 per pound in 2023 to $98.7 by 2033, with prices continuing to edge higher beyond that point.

Revenue: Kazatomprom & Cameco dominate

Despite the growing interest in new uranium supply, the market remains highly concentrated in 2025. Kazakhstan’s NAC Kazatomprom JSC (NATKY) (KAS:KZAP) and Canada’s Cameco Corp. (CCJ) (TSE:CCO) continue to account for the overwhelming majority of revenue and production.

Visible Alpha consensus forecasts show that Kazatomprom is expected to generate about $3.3 billion in uranium revenue in 2025, ahead of Cameco’s roughly $2.1 billion.

Both miners are also heavily geared to the commodity. Uranium accounts for about 91% of Kazatomprom’s total revenue and around 83% of Cameco’s, leaving earnings particularly sensitive to price swings and contract renewals.

Beyond the leaders, the market drops off quickly.

  • China’s CGN Mining (CGNMF) (HKG:1164) is expected to post uranium revenues of $253 million in 2025
  • Australia’s Paladin Energy (PALAF) (TSE:PDN) is seen generating $247 million
  • Boss Energy (BQSSF) (ASX:BOE) at $96 million
  • US-based Uranium Energy (UEC) is forecast at roughly $56 million, while
  • Canada’s Denison Mines (DNN) (TSE:DML) is expected to generate just $9 million

Over the longer term, however, supply is set to broaden. Developers such as NexGen Energy (NXE), Deep Yellow (DYLLF) (ASX:DYL), Bannerman Energy (BNNLF) (ASX:BMN), Lotus Resources (LTSRF) (ASX:LOT), which are currently advancing projects through drilling programs and partnerships, are expected to contribute meaningful production within the next three to seven years.

While Kazatomprom and Cameco remain the anchors, the fastest revenue growth is seen among emerging producers including:

  • NextGen, projected to see uranium revenues of $1.3 billion by 2030, up from virtually no revenue realization in 2025
  • Denison Mines, moving from negligible revenue today to $768 million by 2030
  • Uranium Energy, expected to grow from $56 million in 2025 to $548 million by 2030

Production set to surge after 2028

The production outlook tells the same story. Kazatomprom and Cameco’s production dominance is equally striking. Kazatomprom is projected to produce 29.1 million pounds of uranium in 2025, while Cameco is forecast at 21 million pounds. Combined, they account for roughly 86% of total output among the seven producers covered in this analysis in 2025.

The most notable shift in the Visible Alpha projections comes after 2028, when a second wave of producers begins to scale meaningfully. Total uranium production across the group is expected to expand from 58.5 million pounds in 2025 to 141.2 million pounds by 2033. That is a near 2.5x increase in less than a decade.

The incremental supply is driven by developers transitioning into production:

  • NexGen Energy is projected to ramp to more than 14.9 million pounds by 2030
  • Denison Mines rises above 8.5 million pounds by 2030
  • Uranium Energy expands beyond 6.6 million pounds by 2030
  • Australia-based developers including Deep Yellow, Bannerman, Lotus, and Boss Energy also add meaningful volume

Capital spending accelerates

Capital spending (CapEx) is already accelerating. Aggregate uranium capex is expected to rise from $704 million in 2024 to $969 million in 2025, and a peak of $1.6 billion in 2027, before reducing to $1.5 billion in 2028 as big players like Kazatomprom and Cameco scale back investment.

Spending is front-loaded, with developers investing heavily before production begins:

  • NexGen capex rises above $500 million by 2028
  • Denison, along with Lotus Resources, accelerates capex sharply, with triple-digit growth rates in 2025 and 2026
  • Paladin and Deep Yellow are also seen to scale investments

Over time, as the largest producers moderate spending, smaller entrants are expected to take on a greater share of investment.

Price-to-NAV signals a two-tier uranium equity market

Looking at valuation metrics, Visible Alpha consensus net asset value estimates highlight an increasingly segmented market between premium incumbents and discounted developers.

Cameco remains the clear standout, with an attributable NAV of $25.5 billion and a price-to-NAV multiple of 2.3 times relative to peers. Kazatomprom, despite its comparable asset base at $20.7 billion, trades much closer to intrinsic value at just 1.0 times NAV, suggesting geopolitical and jurisdictional risk continue to cap its valuation.

Mid-tier names such as NexGen, Denison, and Uranium Energy sit in the middle, trading between 1.3 and 1.6 times NAV, indicating markets are already pricing in meaningful upside from projects still moving through the buildout phase.

In contrast, smaller Australian developers Deep Yellow and Boss trade below NAV, while Lotus Resources trades at only 0.6 times.

Overall, Visible Alpha projections suggest the uranium market is moving into a fundamentally different phase. 2025 remains defined by tight supply, improving pricing and dominance by major producers. But by the early 2030s, a wave of new production reshapes the supply curve, with output more than doubling and developers turning into meaningful competitors.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Carvana Drops Despite Higher Sales
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Carvana's (CVNA)Q4 profitability loses traction; shares crater more than 20%. (00:14) DoorDash (DASH) sees double-digit growth in orders, GOV; sets upbeat Q1 guidance. (01:31) Moderna (MRNA) gains as FDA backtracks and accepts flu shot for review. (02:14)

This is an abridged transcript.

Carvana (CVNA) sold more cars than Wall Street expected in the fourth quarter, but concerns over the company’s profitability have pushed the stock down this morning.

Carvana is down 16% in premarket action.

The used-car marketplace experienced a 58% surge in revenue.

On a per-unit basis, retail vehicle sales were up 43% while wholesale increased 66%.

However, breaking down the numbers further shows a $244 decline in the gross profit per vehicle (-$354 on an adjusted basis) and an 18.2% decline in wholesale marketplace gross profit.

The company’s profitability gave a mixed picture, as adjusted EBITDA increased to $511M but missed $539M estimates, while on a per-share basis, adjusted profits surged to $4.22 per share from $0.56 per share, although this was attributed to one-time charges and so is not comparable to $1.12 estimates.

The company did not issue sales guidance for the full year but said it continues to expect to sell 3M cars between 2030 and 2035.

DoorDash (DASH) is up 13% in premarket trading.

Shares surged as strong order growth and upbeat guidance outweighed a Q4 top- and bottom-line miss.

Orders climbed 32% to 903M, and GOV jumped 39% to $29.7B, both above expectations.

The Q1 outlook for GOV ($31B–$31.8B) and EBITDA ($675M–$775M) came in strong.

Investors looked past softer margins and heavy investment spending, choosing to focus on demand momentum and growth visibility.

Now we have an update to a story we brought you last week on Wall Street Breakfast.

Moderna (MRNA) closed 6% higher on Wednesday after the U.S. FDA accepted the company’s marketing application for its messenger RNA-based shot mRNA-1010, reversing an earlier stance to refuse its review.

In a press release, the biotech said that the agency has also assigned August 5, 2026, as the target action date in connection with its biologics license application (BLA) for mRNA-1010.

Shares of Moderna came under pressure last week after the FDA issued a refusal-to-file letter, indicating that its biologics unit, which regulates vaccines, would not review the BLA.

The company has submitted an amended BLA after proposing a revised regulatory path for the shot during a Type A meeting with the FDA. Changes include plans to seek an age-dependent regulatory pathway and a post-marketing requirement to conduct an additional trial in older adults.

Moderna (MRNA) expects to launch mRNA-1010 in the U.S. for adults aged 50 years and older for the 2026/2027 flu season.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha

GameStop's Ryan Cohen takes on the 'hollow men' of corporate boardrooms

Shift away from EVs continues to cost Avis Budget

Figma targets 30% revenue growth for 2026 as AI adoption accelerates

Catalyst watch:

  • A10 Networks (ATEN) will host its Investor Day event in San Francisco. The leadership team will outline A10’s long-term growth framework across legacy networking, next-generation networking, and security.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in the red. Crude oil is up 1% at $65/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.6% at $66,000. Gold is up 0.2% at $4,985.

The FTSE 100 is down 0.8% and the DAX is down 0.8%. Chinese and Hong Kong markets were closed for the Lunar New Year holiday.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Figma (FIG) +15% - Shares jumped after strong Q4 results and upbeat guidance.

Economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Jobless Claims

  • 10:00 am Leading Indicators

data.title: Lending Standards Ease - Constructive Signal For Commercial Real Estate
data.description:
By Mike Sobolik, Investment Strategist, Direct Real Estate, North America

US commercial real estate (CRE) got an encouraging signal. Banks have begun to ease underwriting standards for CRE loans for the first time since interest rates started rising in 2022, according to the January 2026 Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey (SLOOS) from the Federal Reserve. It suggests lenders are gaining confidence that market conditions have stabilized and that risks are becoming more manageable.

This shift toward easing is expected to boost CRE loan origination activity and support property transaction growth, broadening investment opportunities within CRE equity and credit. Similar turns in lending standards have historically tended to occur early in recovery phases, often marking the start of a new capital cycle. Comparable patterns followed the tech downturn in the early 2000s, the post–Global Financial Crisis period beginning in 2011, and the economic reopening after the COVID-19 era stimulus in 2021–2022. (See the chart below.) The January 2026 SLOOS reported a net tightening of -3.16% (negative numbers reflect net easing) across combined CRE loan types, including construction and land development loans, loans secured by nonfarm, nonresidential properties (e.g., office, retail, industrial), and multi-family loans. Anything less than zero means underwriting standards are easing; greater than zero means underwriting standards are tightening.

Banks start to ease commercial real estate underwriting standards

Tightening standards, all CRE loans, net percentage of banks (%, not seasonally adjusted, axis inverted)

Sources: Invesco Real Estate, utilizing data from Moody’s Analytics and the Federal Reserve Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey (SLOOS), as of Feb. 2, 2026. The relationship between the SLOOS results and commercial real estate capital returns is based on a -0.789 correlation between the net percentage of banks tightening loan standards and one-year capital property-level returns of the NCREIF Fund Index - Open-End Diversified Core Equity (NFI-ODCE) Index two quarters later from Q1-2000 to Q4-2025. NFI-ODCE is a fund-level capitalization-weighted, time-weighted return index and includes property investments at ownership share, cash balances, and leverage. Investments cannot be made directly in an index. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results.

While modest, if this shift into net easing continues into future quarters, it could support a gradual improvement in commercial property values and movement into a virtuous cycle of more CRE transactions and opportunity. While loan originations are expected to grow broadly across lender types, market share over recent years has moved up significantly for private non-bank CRE lenders, which averaged 4.6% of total CRE loan originations from 2010–2020. Post-pandemic, their share averaged 8.6% from 2021 through Q3-2025.1

Footnotes

1 Source: Invesco Real Estate, utilizing data from Mortgage Bankers Association as of Nov. 6, 2025, most recent data available. Loan origination market share for private non-bank CRE lenders averaged 4.6% of total CRE loan originations from 2010–2020. Post-pandemic, their share has averaged 8.6% from 2021 through Q3-2025.

Important information

NA5216158

Image: GettyImages / Gary Yeowell

All investing involves risk, including the risk of loss.

Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Investments cannot be made directly in an index.

This does not constitute a recommendation of any investment strategy or product for a particular investor. Investors should consult a financial professional before making any investment decisions.

Investments in real estate-related instruments may be affected by economic, legal, or environmental factors that affect property values, rents, or occupancies of real estate. Real estate companies, including REITs or similar structures, tend to be small- and mid-cap companies, and their shares may be more volatile and less liquid.

Investments focused in a particular sector, such as real estate, are subject to greater risk and are more greatly impacted by market volatility than more diversified investments.

REITs are subject to additional risks than general real estate investments. The value of a REIT can depend on the structure and cash flow generated by it.

REITs concentrated in a limited number or type of properties, investments, or narrow geographic areas are subject to the risks affecting those properties or areas to a greater extent than less concentrated investments. REITs are subject to certain requirements under federal tax law and may have expenses, including advisory and administration expenses.

The Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey on Bank Lending Practices (SLOOS) is a quarterly survey conducted by the Federal Reserve. It surveys senior loan officers at large US banks and US branches of foreign banks about changes in lending standards, loan terms, and borrower demand across major credit categories, including commercial real estate (CRE) loans.

The Federal Reserve reports net percentages for each category individually. Moody’s Analytics publishes a weighted average across the three categories, which is the measure referenced here.

The opinions referenced above are those of the author as of Feb. 10, 2026. These comments should not be construed as recommendations, but as an illustration of broader themes. Forward-looking statements are not guarantees of future results. They involve risks, uncertainties, and assumptions; there can be no assurance that actual results will not differ materially from expectations.

©2026 Invesco Ltd. All rights reserved

Lending Standards Ease - Constructive Signal For Commercial Real Estate by Invesco US

data.title: Delta Air Lines: Deleveraging, Margin Discipline, And Valuation Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my opinion, Delta Air Lines (DAL) is still undervalued, even though the stock price has increased by more than 18.5% since my first article was published in August. The financial results for Q4 turned out to be mixed - the market was disappointed with the revenue growth rate, but it seems to me that this is more likely due to inflated expectations after a strong Q3. Delta remains one of the most profitable companies in the sector, with multiples trading at discounts of 20-30% to the sectoral averages. I have updated the DCF model based on the released data for the entire year 2025 and am raising the target price for the shares to $78 with an upside of 13%. The Buy rating is saved.

Premium Revenue Expansion Offsets Economic Cabin Contraction

By the end of Q4, revenue grew by only 3% yoy and amounted to $16 billion, against market expectations of $15.75 billion. At the same time, excluding refining sales, revenue amounted to $14.61 billion, while the consensus forecast was $14.72 billion. As a result, investors considered that revenue showed a low growth rate and sold off shares after the release of the financial statements. In fact, a 1.2% yoy increase is really not what we would like, but in my opinion this is not a reason to sell shares. Let's look at what happened to the revenue.

According to management comments, the company earned 2% less revenue in Q4 due to the impact of the shutdown. The alleged government shutdown had a negative impact on the domestic transportation segment. To be honest, this is a rather controversial thesis. I don't have specific figures, but it doesn't seem to me that the share of federal employees and some contractors in revenue is so large as to have such a big impact on its growth rate. Otherwise, I do not know how the shutdown can affect revenue. Moreover, Delta increased its capacity (ASK) by 1.3% and managed to keep total revenue per unit capacity (TRASM) at last year's level. This suggests that overall aggregate demand remained stable. In addition, the company records an increase in demand for business tourism - airline ticket sales to corporate customers increased in Q4. Rather, I am inclined to believe that the revenue structure began to change, and this could affect the growth rate. Because the premium segment grew by 9% year-on-year, while the economy class decreased by 7%. The demand for domestic economy class flights is clearly low, and this puts pressure on total revenue. The demand for international destinations turned out to be much higher due to the restoration of transoceanic traffic.

Almost 60% of the company's total revenue is generated by premium products, and the share of economy class is falling further. This is the company's strategy, and it helps it remain one of the most profitable in the sector. Revenue from the sale of premium products increased by 7% yoy. Delta has invested a lot in the configuration of salons, lounges, and the brand, and is now successfully increasing revenue. Revenue from loyalty programs (including the partnership with American Express) increased by +6% yoy, reaching a record $8.2 billion in Amex rewards by the end of 2025. Revenue from MRO services increased by 25%, which is a very good result, as MRO has a highly profitable history that positively affects operating margins. Thus, the company's revenue is gradually becoming less volatile and more margin-efficient, as management has decided to change its business model by relying on premium service.

Operating profit was lower than last year's results - $ 1.47 billion versus $1.72 billion. As a result, the operating margin fell to 9.2% from 11.0%. The adjusted indicator has also decreased. According to the results of Q3, the management itself predicted that the margin would be in the range of 10.5-12%. That's because revenue grew more slowly than operating expenses, which increased by 5% yoy due to rising personnel and fleet maintenance costs following new labor agreements. It could have been even worse, but fuel costs decreased by 1% over the period to $2.4 billion. As a result, for the whole of 2025, the company earned $5.8 billion in operating profit, and the margin was about 10%, which is slightly lower than in 2024. Events did not work out in favor of the company this quarter, although, as I said, strategically, management is moving towards high-margin sales, abandoning low-margin ones. Therefore, I would not categorically react to a weak Q4 result. The situation will start to change gradually.

Net profit pleased shareholders with strong growth compared to 2024. But there is also no need to fall into euphoria; the growth is more likely due to the underestimated profits of 2024 due to last year's losses from the revaluation of investments. At the end of the quarter, net profit amounted to $1.22 billion, which is 45% higher than in Q4 2024. GAAP EPS was $1.86 per share, almost the upper threshold of management's forecast. Adjusted EPS decreased significantly due to a drop in operating profit and amounted to $1.55, with a consensus of $1.52. For the full year, net profit was $5 billion and adjusted EPS was $5.82, which is lower than consensus expectations ($6.0). Of course, the last quarter of the year slightly spoiled the overall picture on a yearly basis. But I would not exaggerate the scale of the disaster. After a strong Q3, expectations were quite high, and the situation in Q4 was not in favor of the company, which resulted in such a result.

2026 Guidance Signals Margin Expansion and Earnings Growth

Management's forecasts for 2026 and Q1 are quite optimistic, despite the results of Q4. Management has already noted record booking volumes in early January and continued demand from passengers, including corporate customers. Throughout the year, the company expects both revenue growth and operating margin growth compared to 2025, which will be facilitated by revenue growth from premium and other high-margin products while controlling costs.

It is interesting to compare the expectations of management and the market. By the end of the year, according to market consensus, the company will earn $66 billion (+4% y/y) and EPS of $7.19 (+23.5% y/y). This is generally close to the upper threshold of the management's forecast. In Q1, the company is expected to earn $14.70 billion (+4.68%y/y) and EPS of $0.71 (+54% y/y). Consensus forecasts mostly fall in the middle of the ranges set by management. This indicates a convergence of views and reduces the risks of severe market disappointment. In my opinion, the given forecasts are quite realistic and feasible. Moreover, management has already announced high demand at the beginning of the year. According to my expectations, the company will earn about $66-63 billion in revenue and $7.17-7.20 EPS in 2026. In Q1, I forecast revenue of $14.67-14.71 and EPS of $0.71-0.73. Let's move on to the fair value assessment and DCF model.

Discounted Cash Flow Signals Compelling Entry Point

In this chapter, I will talk about my DCF model, its prerequisites, and final evaluation. Let me remind you that I calculate the target share price by forecasting the unlevered FCF for the period 2026-2030, discounting the forecast FCFS, then calculating the terminal value by multiplying the forward EV/EBITDA and P/E by EBITDA and net profit of the last forecast year (2030) and also discounting. I add up the discounted FCF and Terminal Value to get the total value of the business (when calculated using P/E, this is immediately the Market cap. With EV/EBITDA, this is the Enterprise value from which Net Debt is also subtracted), which I divide by the number of shares and get the projected fair value of one share.

The main prerequisites of the model have been preserved - you can read about them in detail in the last article. I expect the revenue CAGR of 2026-2030 to be 3.70%, a balanced growth in a competitive market. The operating margin will grow to 11.5-12% due to the strategic transition to selling more premium products, but I do not expect a stronger margin growth, as costs will also increase, and competitors will not allow you to earn more. Capital expenditures will be kept in the range of 8-10%, which will allow updating the fleet and maintaining the infrastructure. As a result, the average FCF yield in 2026-2030 will be about 4.68%, while for the period 2021-2025 the figure was 3.05%. In fact, my thesis is that changing the business model will allow us to increase margins and, consequently, increase free cash flow.

Delta's WACC has increased since the last article from 7.20% to 7.53% due to an increase in the risk-free rate and a five-year beta to 1.34x (the remaining prerequisites are listed in the table above).

The company is fundamentally undervalued by the market. Compared to the sectoral averages by multiples, it seems that Delta is expected to go bankrupt. The discounts average 50-60%. Even with the increase in the share price that was in 2025, the discounts are still huge. This is despite the fact that in terms of revenue growth, Delta is somewhere in the middle compared to peers, and in terms of business profitability, it is generally one of their best. The shares are clearly oversold, and with the gradual improvement of financial results, these multiples are bound to decrease.

In the basic scenario, both methods give an upside in the range of 12-13%. In the most positive scenario (growth in P/E and EV/EBITDA and a further reduction in WACC), the stock may increase in price by 25-30%. In a negative scenario, the downside is limited to -2.5%. Thus, I maintain a Buy rating with a target price of $78, with an upside of 13% to the current price in the baseline scenario.

Strengthening Financial Position Supports Capital Returns

The debt burden has decreased over the year and is under management control. At the end of 2025, the total debt amounted to $21.4 billion, with about $4.3 billion in accounts, respectively, net debt amounted to $17 billion. The Debt/EBITDA ratio has decreased from 3.11x to 2.88x since 2024, and Net Debt/EBITDA from 2.79x to 2.49x. Operating profit exceeds interest expenses by 10 times. 95% of the total debt has a fixed rate, and the weighted average rate on all debts is 4.3%.

With a stable balance sheet and high free cash flow, the company tries to pay both dividends and buy back shares from the market. In 2025, the quarterly dividend was increased from $0.15 to $0.1875. In total, the company sent investors $440 million in dividends with a yield of about 1%. There were no share buybacks last year, but according to management, the company is ready to resume this process.

Risks

The company's risks are mainly related to the demand for air travel, both domestically and internationally, and the deterioration of the macroeconomic situation may negatively affect this demand. Volatility in fuel prices can also lead to higher operating costs and lower margins. Delta owns its own oil refinery, and this helps to smooth out the impact of price fluctuations, but this does not always help either. High competition in the industry can also put pressure on margins. There are also operational risks such as IT failures, personnel problems, or weather events that can temporarily disrupt the schedule and affect results.

Final Thoughts

In general, 2025 was quite a successful year for the company, in my opinion. Yes, Q4 messed up the overall picture a bit, but I'm inclined to consider it an unfortunate set of circumstances. I like strategic business development, and the numbers in the reports show that management is quite successful in achieving its goals. So far, not all the effects of the business model change have fully manifested themselves. At the same time, Delta shares are fundamentally undervalued and oversold. Over time, this discount in the share price relative to peers will close as financial results become sustainably strong. I'm keeping a buy on Delta shares for the next year.

data.title: Emerging Market Corporates: Renowned For Resilience
data.description:
Emerging Market Corporates Have Been Resilient Across Market Cycles

Emerging-market (EM) corporates have a track record of resilience across market cycles. For over a decade, EM corporate bonds have allowed for participation in rising markets while exposing investors to less downside during market downdrafts (Display, left). This is due to an especially pronounced inherent barbell structure that balances interest-rate risk with credit risk, providing independent sources of return that are negatively correlated to each other (Display, right).

This dynamic comes naturally to EM corporates. EM corporate debt priced in US dollars is sensitive to US interest rates. This helps provide a defensive buffer against volatile markets. Meanwhile, the sector’s lower-rated credits boost upside participation in risk-on environments. (While the EM corporate market is rated investment-grade in aggregate, it reflects the entire credit spectrum, from high-grade through high-yield debt.)

This natural barbell feature also partly explains why the EM corporate market’s Sharpe ratio, or risk-adjusted return, for more than 10 years has been meaningfully higher than that of both US investment-grade corporates and the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index.

That’s why we believe EM corporate bonds’ balance of interest rates and credit can help prepare investors for whatever conditions lie ahead.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Madison Square Garden: A Potential Spin-Off And A Vanishing Discount
data.description:
The Unthinkable Might Finally Happen

Madison Square Garden Sports Corp. (NYSE:MSGS) is a unique small-cap company that allows shareholders to own a piece of professional US sports. The stock encompasses equity ownership of both the NBA's New York Knicks and the NHL's New York Rangers. There aren't many other opportunities to do this, with the Atlanta Braves Holdings, Inc. (BATRK) being the other often owned and written about opportunity.

Some news on the recent announcement:

MSG Sports, in part following activist shareholder pressure, is exploring a spin-off of the NBA’s Knicks and NHL’s Rangers into two separate, publicly traded companies.

The James Dolan–led entity said Wednesday that it is looking into the move and whether it would provide “enhanced strategic and financial flexibility,” though there is no certainty it will happen and no defined timetable for a decision.

“Both the Knicks and Rangers are premier teams in their respective leagues, with storied histories and large and passionate fan bases,” Dolan, MSG Sports executive chairman and CEO, said in a statement. “We believe this proposed transaction would provide each company with enhanced strategic flexibility, its own defined business focus, and clear characteristics for investors.”

The formal exploration of the spin-off arrives nearly eight months after activist investor Boyar Value Group pressured MSG Sports to do this, arguing that the full value of the Knicks was particularly trapped in the current corporate structure.

Investors in this stock have seen value for years. Two of the most valuable franchises in their respective leagues are being sold at a discount to comps. However, they had little faith that ownership and CEO James Dolan would budge and split apart the two companies to make them marketable. The Dolan family controls 3 companies currently:

Company Ticker Owns
Madison Square Garden Sports MSGS Knicks, Rangers
Madison Square Garden Entertainment (MSGE) MSG Arena, Radio City
Sphere Entertainment (SPHR) Sphere Las Vegas

I wrote about MSGS for the first time back in June of 2025. It took a while to finally get rolling, but it now seems to have gained some popularity.

I admit I, personally, exited too early, as my conservative price target was under the prices we are hitting today. As more and more hedge fund managers, tech CEOs, and retiring big execs hunt for legacy assets, sports franchises are right in the crosshairs. If you asked me personally, and you gave me $10 billion dollars, I would gladly spend most of it on the San Francisco 49ers and enjoy the rest of my days hanging out with my family in the owner's box.

An extra couple of billion in the market left over after buying the team is all I'd need. Break even to a slight profit on the franchise with the knowledge I own one of the premier teams in a premier metro location would be a good cherry on the top of a successful life. That's just me; I'm a fanatic, but so are a lot of corporate power brokers and exiting owners.

Did New York Politics Force Their Hand?

This is another question I had in the back of my mind rolling into the NBA season for this company. Would a new New York mayor, in Zohran Mamdani, push New York business owners to sell businesses? Businesses that they may not have considered selling in the past? The first self-proclaimed 'Democratic Socialist' might scare some owners around the city. If someone was looking for the final little push to sell if they believed the business environment was about to change, this might be it.

What is the Dolan Discount?

Back to the Dolan discount. Because shareholders typically viewed the entity as unsellable due to having two teams combined, there was always a discount involved due to the ownership's unwillingness to explore the market and consider a spin-off. This discount was in the neighborhood of 30-40% of fair market value.

The Valuation

First off, enterprise value has risen quite a bit since my last article [up to $8.17 Billion from just over $5]. The total long-term debt piece is largely made up of lease obligations to the stadium, which, in the prior article, I simply backed out the stadium value from the valuations of both teams to estimate value. I am assuming here that the stadium, owned by Madison Square Garden Entertainment, not MSGS, would not be included in a deal for a minority or controlling stake.

The Knicks and Rangers have both been updated on their valuations for the year-end. I will run the exact same valuation estimate that I did in my prior article with updated assumptions:

  • $9.75 Billion NY Knicks
  • $4 Billion NY Rangers
  • Minus $1.865 X 2, subtracting $3.73B as $1.865 Billion is included in each team's Forbes valuation.
  • Potential fair market value = ($9.75+$4)-$3.73 Billion = $10.02 billion
  • Current Enterprise Value= $8.178 Billion
  • Selling at 81.6% of possible enterprise value.
  • Divided by 24.1 million shares outstanding = $414.93/share FMV
  • At a 30% Dolan Discount = $290.45/share
  • At a 40% Dolan Discount= $248.96/share
  • Average= $269.7

I ran the exact same average of the valuations at 30% and 40% off the estimated market value to get to $269.7. The question here for new buyers remains: Do you think the Dolan discount is gone? Is it ok to start looking at the future of possible sales of each individual team? Even if it's not a complete buyout, the sale of a controlling stake could put the valuation much higher, depending on what price is paid.

Summary

When I wrote the prior article, the discount was so steep that the stock was selling well below the Dolan discount price. I estimate it's above that now [well above that], but still below what the actual market value probably is. Investors also have to ask themselves how effective the activist investor group Boyar Value will be in pushing this team division through to the finish. Also, consider that the New York business environment might be changing to push some NYC business owners toward considering sales.

I, personally, like deep discounts to have a margin of safety. I don't think that margin is there any longer. I consider the stock a hold at this point, with investors now anticipating a sale and not hedging for a possible reconsideration by the Dolan team in the other direction [not splitting and not selling].

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Autodesk: Pre-Earnings Assessment Of 'SaaSpocalypse'
data.description:
In the 8-K released on January 22nd, Autodesk (ADSK) (the company) gave the public some information that will be quite impactful to its stockholders. The company is proceeding with its sales and marketing restructuring, which started with its change in how billings are recorded and accounted for, and now culminated in the layoff of 7% of its workers, as detailed in the 8-K. The company also gave some information about guidance and some tax expenses, which will be further discussed in the coming paragraphs.

Autodesk is a company with a well-known legacy product portfolio that almost every engineer and architect has used in their careers. Their software portfolio includes monsters like AutoCAD, Revit, Civil 3D, Forma, Autodesk Fusion, Inventor Professional, Vault, Moldflow, Advance Steel, Robot Structural Analysis, TinkerCad, Flame, Flow, and more. These tools aid professionals in architecture, engineering, and construction (AEC), manufacturing, media, and more. As detailed in their FY 2025 annual report, 97% of total net revenue is comprised of recurring revenue generated by their subscription. The revenue decomposition by product groups can be seen below.

Apart from the standard revenue decomposition, I have also added the expected CAGR for the next 4-5 years according to industry professionals and financial institutions. The AEC segment growth is mostly in line with the overall construction industry; the expected growth from Deloitte's Construction Report was used as the base, where they expect the industry to grow to $16.11 trillion in 2030 compared to $11.39 trillion in 2024. For AutoCAD and AutoCAD LT, the overall SaaS industry trend was taken as a base, and the forecasts by Statista Market Insights were used, where they expect the industry to reach $887.05 billion in 2030 compared to the $428.78 billion in 2025. For the manufacturing segment, again, a forecast by Statista Market Insights was used, where they expect the industry to reach $54.11 trillion in 2029 compared to $52.32 trillion in 2025. For the M&E segment, the forecast by PwC has been used, where they expect the industry to reach $3.5 trillion by 2029, compared to $2.93 trillion in 2024. Finally, for the other segments, I have used a conservative overall economic growth rate of 2.5%.

Using these industry forecasts and comparing them to analyst consensus revenues per year, it is possible to get some more information on what the market expects from Autodesk. As it can be seen, there is a consistent growth gap between what the analysts expect from Autodesk and the weighted growth by industry growth rate, where the industry-weighted CAGR is 7.89%, the consensus revenue growth is 12.67%, thus resulting in a 4.43% residual additional CAGR attributable to other factors.

What are the other factors? The two that come to mind are that analysts expect different industry growth trends and/or Autodesk to gain market share. Accounting for the fact that overall industry growth trends cannot be that wildly different, I would mostly attribute it to market share gains and pricing power. According to the latest 10-Q filed November 26th, Autodesk has generated $5,249 million in the 9 months ended, and in the Q3 investor presentation, they have guided a revenue range of $7,150-7,165 million. In the latest 8-K already discussed in the article, the company stated that they believe Autodesk will post revenue that is in the upper range of guidance, as business went very well.

Looking at the expected 2026 revenue expectation realization probability, which is quite high due to factors already discussed above, and Autodesk's investments into AI, sales & marketing reconstruction, and the overall moat and power that the company holds thanks to its legacy, it seems quite probable that the company will be able to at least match analyst expectations.

Although revenue growth is expected to be in the high range, there are also some negative effects that may influence EPS. In the same 8-K already discussed, it is said that the restructuring charges of approximately $135 to $165 million are expected to be attributable to employee termination benefits, of which $90 to $110 million is attributed to Q4 of FY 2026. Although this may create some drops in profitability in the short term, the headcount reduction will most probably increase margins and thus profitability. It is also noted that the savings from the headcount reduction will be funneled into "expanding AI, platform, and industry cloud leadership", as well as overall corporate investments, and thus culminating in the competition of the go-to-market transformation.

Although much "philosophy" is put onto it, the reasons for doing this seem quite simple. Increase investments without damaging margins. S&P Global has given Autodesk a credit rating of BBB+, and this rating has been sustained since 2022. As detailed in the article, an increase in the leverage (debt to EBITDA), or volatility in operating margins may prompt a lowering of the credit rating. Thus, the company cannot take on excessive debt or sacrifice margins for its goal of investing in these projects. Thus, I believe they followed the easiest and most obvious way to increase their "budget" while keeping margins stable, which was to lay off a part of their workforce.

To understand how this layoff may affect the company and to also get some more information about the company, I find it important to read the reviews that were left on Glassdoor. The company has an overall 70% positive business outlook, with unlimited PTO, positive work culture, and remote-first culture being the top positive points. The negative reviews touch upon anxieties that the company may have developed a "layoff culture" as a way of optimization, the fact that there are too many layers of management that make progress slower, and the fact that the company seems mostly focused on sales and acquires innovation through acquisitions rather than developing it in-house.

These reviews are extremely important to the outlook of the company, as they directly relate to the overall investor sentiment around the company. There were some reviews about how AI has created a "tech debt," and the company is lagging behind its competitors. These reviews further reinforce the view that the layoffs may have been done as a means to innovate while keeping margins healthy, rather than a simple efficiency-related layoff round, and may even point towards future M&A activity.

Finally, let's look at Autodesk's stance against its peers. I have decided that Adobe, Bentley, Ansys, and Procore are suitable peers of Autodesk, as they're affected by similar systematic factors and/or are present in the same business lines.

Autodesk has fallen 25.98% from its 52-week high, which is less than the mean of the peer group, which dropped by 45.48%. In addition, its market beta is 1.45 compared to the group mean of 1.22. This shows that although Autodesk was hit by the SaaS selloff that has been going on in the market for a while now, it has held better than its peers, even if it has a more cyclical nature, as can be inferred from the higher beta.

Autodesk's trailing multiples, such as its P/E (47.29), P/S (7.52), EV/EBITDA (34.86), and EV/Sales (7.58), are all higher than the peer group mean (32.23, 6.5, 22.7, and 6.65, respectively). While its forward-looking ratios, such as the FWD P/E (21.04) and PEG (1.29), are around the mean and the median of the peer group, whose means are 21.08 and 1.56, respectively. In addition, the company for the last 5 years has grown its EPS by 39.67% vs. the group mean of 20.76%, but grew sales by 13.42% vs. the mean of 16.94% (although the median is exactly 13.42%), and is expected to grow its EPS by 16.29% compared to the peer mean of 16.05% and median of 12.9%. From this, it is understood that Autodesk's superior EPS growth can be attributed to margin expansion and that its premium valuation can also be attributed to better growth expectations. Although due to the growth outlook not being that much superior to its peers, the valuation may be slightly fragile.

Autodesk has better capital profitability compared to its peers, with an ROA of 10.42% vs. 9.84%, an ROE of 40.33% vs. 26.55%, and an ROIC of 19.95% vs. 15.25%. Autodesk has a slightly higher gross margin of 84.96% compared to the peer mean of 82.72%, but a lower operating margin of 18.97% vs. 21.57% and a lower profit margin of 16.17% vs. 18.44%. Although capital efficiency is indeed important, the inferiority in profitability margins is more concerning. But, it may also be argued that due to the recurring nature of revenues, stable but low margins are acceptable overall, and in addition, the company is on track to improving its margins as already discussed in the article.

Autodesk has a lower current & quick ratio of 0.82 compared to the peer mean of 0.97, but it's not really concerning, while its debt/equity ratio of 0.95 against the peer average of 0.6 is quite substantial. This again may be the result of the luxury of having such a high portion of the revenues as recurring subscription-based revenues, but it does also decrease overall operational leverage.

The company seems to have some part of its premium arrive from its brand value, as it was already discussed that their software portfolio is made of core staples, and from slightly higher future growth potential. The premium is not an unreasonable amount, and thus I conclude that the price is around fair value even if there are some inferior points in the company.

Risks

One of the most cited risks for SaaS companies has been the threat of AI creating similar software that may compete with the businesses and thus erode their moats. Although there is a non-zero chance, I find it highly unlikely. My belief is that AI will be complementary to SaaS companies and actually aid the legacy companies with a vast professional customer base, such as Autodesk.

Although Autodesk is a legacy company with software that covers ample areas of need, the company may fail to innovate and keep pace with new needs, thus falling behind its competitors. I find this risk quite probable, as it is the case with many legacy companies, to fall into the trap of arrogance, leading to overpricing and other actions that lead to customer backlash and the search for alternatives, like in the case of Adobe.

The company may overspend on projects that do not add much value, such as pet AI projects that do not result in any real value while costing large amounts of capital and headcount. I find this risk quite probable too, as the company is already firing employees to invest in AI-related projects that are quite ambiguous.

Conclusion

Although Autodesk is positioned to gain from macro trends such as the infrastructure buildout increase that has been a prevalent theme for a while, overall positive industry trends, a large moat due to its legacy portfolio of products, and a splendid recurring revenue structure, its premium valuation, together with the risks associated with the company, make the company not worthy of a buy rating. Due to my expectations of slight multiple expansion in SaaS companies after the negative sentiment started by Claude gets forgotten, and the probable margin expansion by Autodesk, I have decided to rate Autodesk at Hold.

data.title: The Next Big Theme: February 2026
data.description:
Artificial Intelligence

OpenAI Signals AI Monetization Momentum

OpenAI’s recent research highlights clear, quantifiable evidence of AI monetization momentum, showing how usage and revenue have tracked closely with expanded compute capacity — a key metric linking adoption to commercial growth. Over the past three years, OpenAI’s compute capacity scaled roughly 3x year-over-year, growing from 0.2 gigawatts (GW) in 2023 to 0.6 GW in 2024 and an estimated 1.9 GW in 2025. The company’s revenue followed the same trajectory, rising from about $2 billion in 2023 to $6 billion in 2024 and over $20 billion estimated in 2025. This alignment demonstrates that as more compute became available, more users and enterprises adopted OpenAI’s products and generated revenue through consumer subscriptions, team plans, and usage-based APIs tied directly to outcomes delivered. The company emphasizes that monetization scales with real value delivered, meaning pricing grows only as models become more capable and integrated into workflows, a powerful signal of increasing monetization visibility and robust demand for AI intelligence across use cases.1

Defense Technology

Record U.S. Defense Spending Proposal Prioritizes Advanced Tech

U.S. defense could enter a new expansion phase under the Trump administration’s proposal for a roughly 50% increase in the fiscal year 2027 military budget. At roughly $1.5 trillion, the plan prioritizes modernizing military capabilities, including advanced weapons systems, missile defense, space and cyber capabilities, shipbuilding, and next-generation aircraft. The proposal could also incentivize defense contractors to prioritize production capacity and industrial investment as part of a push to strengthen the domestic defense industrial base.2 The budget increase would further widen the gap between U.S. defense spending and that of other major powers, reinforcing America’s position as the world’s largest military spender. While still subject to legislative approval, the proposal underscores the growing strategic importance of defense, aerospace, and national security technologies, and the potential long-term implications for contractors, supply chains, and capital allocation across the defense tech sector.3

Autonomous & Electric Vehicles

U.S. Robotaxi Market Advances as Waymo Expands Its Footprint

Alphabet’s Waymo officially launched its fully driverless robotaxi service in Miami, expanding into its sixth U.S. city and strengthening its positioning in autonomous ride-hailing. The initial 60-square-mile service area covers highly populated neighborhoods, though not South Beach just yet. The company plans to expand to highways and Miami International Airport later this year. About 10,000 area residents have signed up for rides, which they will hail through Waymo’s app. The company will invite new users gradually before rolling out broader public access. The launch leverages years of testing and an extensive safety record across other cities, where Waymo has logged millions of autonomous miles and demonstrated a lower serious-injury rate than human drivers. The launch also intensifies competition in the autonomous services space as rivals like Tesla and Zoox advance their own robotaxi programs. Waymo plans to expand to more U.S. and international cities as its network scales.4

U.S. Infrastructure

Infrastructure Planning Momentum Is Building

The Dodge Momentum Index, a leading indicator of nonresidential construction planning that typically precedes actual construction spending by 12–18 months, grew 7% in December 2025 to 296.8, up from November’s revised reading of 277.4. Both commercial planning (+3.5%), which includes nonresidential projects like warehouses and office buildings, and institutional planning (+14.9%), which includes public buildings like hospitals, contributed to the monthly gain. For the full year, the index finished 37% higher than in 2024, with commercial planning up 35% and institutional up 43%. December’s year-over-year growth was even stronger at 50% higher than December 2024, underscoring broad expansion across segments. The data center industry remains a notable structural driver, with U.S. data center construction approaching $45 billion on an annualized basis. Forecasts suggest that nonresidential construction starts will continue accelerating through 2027, supported by sustained planning momentum and inflationary pressures that bolster nominal activity despite economic risks.5

AI Semiconductors

AI Demand Is Rewriting the Memory Market

The rapid expansion of AI infrastructure has demand for AI-optimized memory such as high-bandwidth memory (HBM) accelerating sharply. Manufacturers shifting capacity toward higher-value AI workloads has tightened supply in legacy Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM), supporting healthier pricing and improved industry economics. Average selling prices for conventional DRAM have increased in 2026. At the same time, elevated investment in AI-driven memory technologies reinforces long-term growth opportunities for suppliers across training and inference workloads. While capacity adjustments will take time, the current dynamic underscores how AI is reshaping memory demand and improving visibility into future pricing and profitability.6 Overall, AI-led demand tailwinds are driving stronger utilization, better pricing power, and a structurally more favorable outlook for the memory market and the broader semiconductor ecosystem.

Lithium & Battery Tech

U.S. Lithium Processing Capacity Takes a Leap Forward

Tesla began operations at what it describes as the first major lithium refinery in the United States, an important step for the domestic lithium and battery technology ecosystem. Located near Corpus Christi, Texas, the Tesla Lithium Refinery processes spodumene ore into battery-grade lithium hydroxide by using a high-efficiency refining process designed to lower costs and emissions. Roughly two years after groundbreaking, the refinery comes online as demand for lithium continues to rise alongside global electric vehicle adoption and grid-scale storage build-out. Strategically, it should help reduce reliance on overseas lithium processing, strengthening supply chain resilience. The project underscores accelerating investment across the lithium value chain—from raw materials to downstream processing—reinforcing long-term growth prospects for localized battery infrastructure and the lithium producers, refiners, and battery tech providers tied to electrification.7
